codeReview.summary: The event loop dispatch is well-structured with correct priority ordering and a sound fallback strategy for timer+input coincidence. The initial frame correctly uses build_editor_scene, and the scroll-change promotion logic correctly escalates cursor-only moves to document content updates when scrolling is needed. One non-blocking bug found: click-to-move-cursor via tablet input routes to update_selection (which doesn't update cursor x/y), leaving the cursor visually stale until the next event. The worker identified a related issue (MSG_POINTER_ABS redundant update_cursor) but missed this cursor-position-on-click variant. Overall the dispatch logic is correct for the keyboard-only primary path, and the bug self-corrects on the next event.

issues.description: Click (MSG_POINTER_BUTTON) sets changed=true and selection_changed=true but NOT text_changed. When click doesn't cause scroll, dispatch routes to update_selection(), which truncates old selection rects and marks N_CURSOR changed but does NOT update N_CURSOR's x/y coordinates. Result: after a click-to-reposition, the cursor remains at its old screen position until the next keystroke or timer tick triggers a full rebuild. CURSOR_POS is correctly updated in the data model; only the scene graph node is stale. Fix: either (a) add cursor x/y update inside update_selection, (b) route click events to update_document_content by also setting text_changed, or (c) have the dispatch call update_cursor before update_selection when both changed and selection_changed are set.

MSG_POINTER_ABS (mouse move without click) sets changed=true with no other flag. This routes to update_cursor(), which copies front→back, recomputes cursor position from unchanged CURSOR_POS, and swaps — producing a frame with no visible change. Harmless but wastes a copy_front_to_back+swap cycle per mouse move event. Worker correctly identified this in discoveredIssues.

update_document_content receives all build_editor_scene parameters (chrome_bg, chrome_border, chrome_title_color, chrome_clock_color, bg_color) even though its primary path only touches N_DOC_TEXT, N_CURSOR, and selection rects. These are needed solely for the 75%-data-usage fallback to build_editor_scene. Consider extracting a SceneParams struct to reduce parameter count and make the fallback dependency explicit.

The scroll-offset condition `(changed || text_changed)` is redundant — in the current code, text_changed is only ever set when changed is also set (or inside this block which requires changed). The `|| text_changed` is defensive and harmless but could be simplified to just `changed`.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The os-worker skill's visual verification section should document the double-buffer constraint: each targeted update method (update_clock, update_cursor, update_selection, update_document_content) does its own copy_front_to_back + swap cycle, so calling two targeted methods sequentially overwrites the first's change list. This is why the dispatch falls back to full rebuild when timer_fired coincides with other events. The worker identified this in skillFeedback.suggestedChanges.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff skillFeedback.suggestedChanges: 'The os-worker skill's visual verification section could mention that when timer_fired coincides with other updates, calling multiple targeted update methods in sequence overwrites the first's change list'

sharedStateObservations.observation: AGENTS.md documents well-known node indices and the data buffer strategy but doesn't document the dispatch priority order (context_switched > text_changed > selection_changed > changed > timer_fired) or the full_rebuild fallback rule (timer + any input = full rebuild). This is the core logic of the incremental pipeline and would help future workers understand the event loop.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: system/services/core/main.rs lines 843-857: dispatch priority chain with detailed comments, but this logic isn't reflected in AGENTS.md's 'Architecture Context' section

summary: Pass. The dispatch logic correctly routes events to targeted scene graph update methods with appropriate fallbacks. Initial frame uses full build_editor_scene. Timer-only events use update_clock. Cursor-only moves use update_cursor. Text changes use update_document_content with a 75%-data-usage fallback to full rebuild. Timer+input coincidence correctly falls back to full rebuild to avoid the double-swap problem. One non-blocking bug: click-to-reposition-cursor via tablet doesn't update cursor x/y in the scene graph (routes to update_selection which misses cursor position). This self-corrects on the next event. Worker followed TDD procedure, ran all verification steps (1489 tests pass, integration passes, visual verification with screenshots), and correctly identified the MSG_POINTER_ABS redundant update issue.
